bull_thesis: Eaton is the broadest-stack, best-positioned electrical incumbent in the AI build-out, and the data supports that cleanly. First-quarter 2026 revenue came in at $7.5 billion, up 17% year over year with 10% organic growth, alongside a record segment profit of $1.7 billion. The forward-visibility signals are the strongest in the basket: data-center orders up 240% year over year, data-center revenue up 50%, total backlog up 44%, Electrical backlog up 48%, Electrical Americas backlog at $14.5 billion, and a book-to-bill ratio of 1.2 — meaning orders are still running ahead of a record backlog. Management puts a number on the runway: Eaton's equipment goes into roughly 70% of the 32 gigawatts of U.S. data-center capacity currently under construction, with a multi-year backlog at current build rates. The thesis has also widened: the March 2026 acquisition of Boyd Thermal, combined with the NVIDIA DSX 'grid-to-chip' platform, makes Eaton an end-to-end supplier of power and thermal management for AI facilities — from the grid connection all the way through liquid cooling — widening both wallet-share and the cost a customer would bear to switch to a competitor. Demand rests on three independent legs: data centers, reshoring and megaprojects (project announcements up 29% year over year, starts doubled to $54 billion), and a multi-decade utility and grid reinvestment cycle that feeds directly into Eaton's distribution and data-center business. The moat (the durable competitive advantage) is wide: scale, a large installed base, decades of utility and industrial specification lock-in, and high qualification and lead-time barriers in switchgear and high-voltage transformers. Return on invested capital runs roughly 15 percentage points above the cost of capital and is rising on volume. Full-year 2025 free cash flow was $3.6 billion, supporting $1.9 billion of share buybacks and a growing dividend. Full-year guidance was raised by 200 basis points (two percentage points) on organic growth, to 10%, with adjusted earnings per share guided to $13.28 at the midpoint, even after absorbing dilution from the Boyd deal. The dip in Electrical Americas margin — operating profit up only 2% on 20% higher sales — reflects volume-ramp costs and a temporary lag between input-cost increases and the April 2026 price increases that offset them; both have identifiable fixes and are a transitional cost of capturing the order surge, not a sign of structural erosion. What must stay true for this thesis to hold: AI capital spending remains durable through 2027, Electrical Americas margins recover sequentially in the second and third quarters, and the backlog converts at expected rates without mass hyperscaler cancellations.
bear_thesis: You are paying roughly 41 times trailing earnings, or about 30 times forward earnings (the market's consensus estimate for the next twelve months), for a company growing organically at 10% — and the entire marginal upside rests on the same single bet as the rest of the basket: whether AI capital spending holds up. That spending accounts for roughly 40% of S&P 500 earnings growth in 2026 and is the dominant shared risk factor across the entire tape. The optionality is substantially pre-paid: Eaton is the best-in-class name precisely because the market already knows it, which means there is little mispricing left to harvest and the re-rating has largely already happened. The one hard fact the bulls must explain away is uncomfortable: Electrical Americas grew sales by 20% but operating profit rose only 2% — near-zero incremental margin — and management cut the full-year segment-margin guide by 50 basis points. If the 'temporary' framing for this (ramp costs and price-cost lag) proves stickier than advertised — because continuous capacity investment turns out to be structural rather than one quarter's anomaly — then the incremental margins stay compressed and the 30 times multiple is exposed. The 240% data-center order growth also carries real bubble and double-ordering risk: hyperscalers place orders with multiple vendors and can pull back; a single AI capital-spending guide-down (a named risk in the broader regime) would trigger broad de-grossing of every name in the basket simultaneously, including Eaton. The electric-vehicle and Mobility segment is now in retreat, down 6% organically, removing a former growth story and concentrating the thesis on one crowded end-market where Schneider, ABB, Vertiv, and GE Vernova all compete for the same 32 gigawatts. At 30 times forward with no margin of safety, the downside in an AI-capex scare or a recession is a simultaneous multiple contraction and estimate reset — a painful combination. What would confirm the bear: Electrical Americas margins fail to recover sequentially in the second and third quarters, or a hyperscaler cuts its capital-spending plans, or the book-to-bill ratio rolls back through 1.0.
sop_bull: Valuing Eaton as a whole business rather than sum-of-the-parts (the segments are integrated power-management operations, not separable conglomerate divisions). Bull case: full-year 2027 adjusted earnings power of roughly $15.50–$16.00 per share, driven by 10–11% organic compounding, margin recovery toward 24.5%, Boyd and bolt-on accretion, and buyback-driven share-count reduction. Applying a 32 times forward multiple — justified relative to basket peers (Vertiv trades at roughly 44–53 times forward; GE Vernova at roughly 58–71 times forward) for the highest-quality, most-diversified, cash-generative name in the group with a wide moat. 32 times $15.75 equals roughly $505 per share. Bull target: approximately $500–$510 per share, roughly 19% above the current price of $421.77.
sop_bear: Bear case: normalize for AI-capex froth. Full-year 2026 adjusted earnings per share guided to $13.28 at the midpoint; if incremental-margin pressure persists and organic growth decelerates toward 7–8% during a period of capital-spending digestion, 2027 earnings per share falls to roughly $13.75–$14.25. Re-rating to a through-cycle quality-industrial multiple of roughly 22–24 times — still a premium to reflect the wide moat and the secular floor from grid and reshoring spending, but stripping out AI-capex optionality. 23 times $14.00 equals roughly $322 per share. Bear target: approximately $315–$325 per share, roughly 24% below the current price. Floor support: the grid, utility, and reshoring backlog underpins a base earnings stream even if data-center orders halve, so a full de-rating below roughly 20 times is unlikely absent a recession.
sop_breakdown: The base case blends bull and bear: 2027 adjusted earnings power of roughly $14.75–$15.00 per share (10% organic growth moderating, segment margin recovering to roughly 24.3%, Boyd accretion, roughly 1.5% annual share-count reduction from buybacks), multiplied by a roughly 27.5 times forward multiple. That multiple represents a premium to through-cycle industrials but a discount to the hot pure-plays (Vertiv at 44–53 times, GE Vernova at 58–71 times forward), crediting Eaton's wider moat, diversification, and cash returns while reducing for how much AI-capex optionality is already priced in. 27.5 times $14.90 equals roughly $410 per share. The grid, utility, and reshoring backlog provides a hard earnings floor (bear target roughly $320); the data-center order surge provides the upside (bull target roughly $505). Net: at roughly $422, Eaton is trading at or slightly above the base-case estimate of roughly $410 — roughly fairly to fully valued, with no margin of safety.
risk_reward: Roughly symmetric to unfavorable from $421.77: the downside to the bear case is roughly $320 (negative 24%) versus the upside to the bull case of roughly $505 (positive 20%), with a base case of roughly $410 (negative 3%). The reward-to-risk ratio is roughly 0.8 to 1 toward fair value. The asymmetry is negative at the current price — the buyer is paying through the base case for a thesis whose upside requires AI capital spending to keep accelerating, while the downside (a simultaneous multiple contraction and estimate reset on a capital-spending scare) is the basket-wide shared tail risk.
catalyst_status: FIRED / SOFT_EXTENDED. The load-bearing catalysts have largely already PRINTED and re-rated the stock: data-center orders +240% (Q1-26, 2026-05-05), backlog +44%/+48%, book-to-bill 1.2, FY guide raised, NVIDIA DSX partnership and Boyd Thermal close (Mar-2026) are all public and in the price (stock near 52-wk high $435, +10%+ off lows). These are confirmation-of-trend prints, not forward dated binary catalysts. The next catalyst — sequential Electrical Americas margin recovery in Q2-26 (reports ~late-July 2026) — is real and dated but is a SHOW-ME/SOFT driver, not an asymmetric event; a beat is partly expected. There is no spinoff/M&A/regulatory hard-dated re-rating event pending. Verdict: the AI-capex order catalyst is FIRED (already ran); the margin-recovery catalyst is SOFT_EXTENDED (dated but non-binary, partly priced).
peer_comps_note: True scale-out Grid and electrical peers, with live multiples verified 2026-06-22. Eaton trades at roughly 41 times trailing and roughly 30 times forward earnings, with a market capitalization of $164 billion, 10% organic growth, roughly 15% return on invested capital, roughly 22.7% segment margin, and a wide-moat diversified-incumbent profile. Vertiv (ticker: VRT) trades at roughly 44–53 times forward and roughly 75 times trailing earnings at roughly $318 per share — a pure-play data-center power-and-cooling company with roughly 30% organic growth and roughly 51% earnings-per-share growth, the fastest grower at the richest multiple with the narrowest (data-center-only) exposure. GE Vernova (ticker: GEV) trades at roughly 58–71 times forward; first-quarter 2026 orders were up 71% organically in grid, power generation, and electrification — the highest AI-capex sensitivity in the group at the highest multiple. Hubbell (ticker: HUBB) is cheaper at roughly mid-20s forward, with first-quarter adjusted earnings per share of $3.93 (up 16%) and a full-year guide of $19.30–$19.85; it focuses on utility and grid transmission-and-distribution infrastructure with less direct data-center exposure, hence the lower multiple and lower growth. nVent (ticker: NVT) covers data-center enclosures and thermal management at a mid-range multiple. Schneider Electric (Paris-listed) is the largest global electrical franchise with the broadest product range, trading at roughly 20–22 times forward and representing the closest true comparable on breadth, though it is listed in Europe. In the peer context, Eaton sits in the middle of the group on valuation — cheaper than the Vertiv and GE Vernova pure-plays, richer than Hubbell and Schneider — while ranking high on AI-capex exposure (though diversified, not pure-play), at the top on margins, returns, moat quality, and cash generation, and in the middle on growth (10% organic versus Vertiv's 30% or GE Vernova's 71% order growth). It is the quality-at-a-premium choice, not the cheapest or the highest-leverage option.
role_in_scaleout: Eaton is the broad-stack electrical-distribution and power-management backbone of the AI build-out — medium- and low-voltage switchgear, busway, power distribution units, uninterruptible power supplies, transformers, and now (via Boyd Thermal and the NVIDIA DSX partnership) liquid cooling — the grid-to-chip supply chain every hyperscaler must procure regardless of which GPU or optical interconnect wins. Demand durability is high: a multi-year backlog, a book-to-bill of 1.2, and independent grid and reshoring spending legs reinforce it. However, the marginal thesis is tightly coupled to AI capital-spending durability — the same shared driver as the rest of the basket.
consensus_delta: There is no favorable disagreement with the market — and that is the point. The street is not looking the wrong way on Eaton; it is looking directly at it. Twenty-two of twenty-three analysts rate it a buy; the stock is near its 52-week high of $435.43 and trades at roughly 30 times forward earnings, a multiple that already embeds the data-center order surge, backlog growth, and grid and reshoring tailwinds. The edge in a special-situations book comes from the market being offside; here the market is fully onside and the optionality is pre-paid. The only contrarian seam is Electrical Americas margin: the bullish street narrative ('temporary ramp and price-cost lag, recovers in the second and third quarters') could be wrong if continuous capacity investment is structurally dilutive to incremental margins — but that would be a downside delta (the street is too optimistic on margins), not an upside one. The consensus is correct and saturated; positioning with it at 30 times forward offers no expectations arbitrage, and the one place the street might be wrong is to the bear's benefit.
valley_of_death: Eaton has no balance-sheet distress. Net debt stands at roughly $21.3 billion following the debt-funded Boyd Thermal acquisition (up from roughly $10.6 billion at year-end 2025), but against roughly $3.6 billion of free cash flow and roughly $11–12 billion of earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, that is roughly 1.7–1.8 times net leverage — comfortably investment-grade and well-serviced. There is no maturity wall, no cash-burn period, and the dividend is amply covered. The near-term risk over the next three to nine months is entirely about valuation and estimates, not solvency: first, the second-quarter 2026 report (around late July) must show sequential recovery in Electrical Americas margins — if it does not, the 'temporary' narrative breaks and a 30 times multiple de-rates quickly; second, any hyperscaler AI capital-spending guide-down in the second-half 2026 reporting cycle is a named risk factor that would hit Eaton simultaneously with the entire basket; third, a recession or industrial slowdown resets both the multiple and the organic growth estimate. The pain would come before any new catalyst, because the catalysts have already fired — there is no fresh positive event in the three-to-nine-month window to offset a margin miss or a capital-spending scare.
positioning_washout: There is no forced-selling setup here — this is the opposite of a washed-out name. Eaton is a widely held, large-cap S&P 500 constituent, dividend grower, and quality-and-momentum favorite owned by growth, quality, and index funds. It is near its highs with bullish positioning and a consensus buy rating. There is no dividend cut, business pivot, or mechanical liquidation trigger that would create a capitulation entry point. If anything, the risk runs the other way: this is a crowded long in a momentum-favored AI-capex name that could see mechanical selling if the AI-capex factor breaks. The broader regime backdrop — stretched positioning, fund managers' cash levels near 3.9%, and heavy concentration in a handful of large technology names — means there is little dry powder available to absorb a shock. There is no forced-selling entry to buy into; there is positioning crowding to respect.
forcing_function: The next hard date is the second-quarter 2026 earnings report, expected around late July 2026. The binary question is whether Electrical Americas operating margin recovers sequentially off the 25.6% first-quarter print and validates management's 'temporary ramp and price-cost lag' framing. A clean recovery combined with a sustained book-to-bill ratio above 1.1 reaffirms the bull case but is largely expected, limiting the upside re-rating; a second quarter of compressed incremental margins shatters the 'temporary' narrative and forces a de-rating — that asymmetric move is to the downside, not the upside. Secondary catalysts that could force a re-rating: hyperscaler second-half 2026 capital-spending guidance updates (the shared AI-capex factor) and any update on conversion of the 32 gigawatt pipeline. Critically, there is no pending spinoff, acquisition, proxy contest, or regulatory hard catalyst — the re-rating events have already fired, so what remains is a show-me on margins, not a fresh upside trigger.
moderator_conclusion: Eaton is, on the forensics, one of the highest-quality names this committee will see: management credibility 5 of 5, a wide and stable moat, a strengthening business trajectory, a record backlog up 44% with a book-to-bill of 1.2, return on invested capital roughly 15 percentage points above the cost of capital, $3.6 billion of free cash flow, and a genuinely differentiated 'grid-to-chip' position — Boyd Thermal plus the NVIDIA DSX platform — that makes it the broad-stack electrical-and-thermal backbone of the AI build-out. None of that is in question. What the current regime forces us to ask is about timing and saturation, and on both counts Eaton scores poorly for a fresh allocation. The load-bearing catalyst — the data-center order and backlog inflection, with orders up 240% — has already fired and re-rated the stock to roughly 30 times forward earnings near a 52-week high. The optionality is pre-paid. At $421.77 the stock sits roughly at the reconciled base-case fair-value estimate of $410 — reward-to-risk is symmetric to unfavorable, roughly 0.8 to 1, with the downside (roughly $320 on a combined margin-miss and capital-spending-scare reset) being the basket's shared tail: Eaton's marginal thesis depends on the same single macro driver as the rest of Basket B, which is a concentration the regime tells us to penalize. Layer on the one real bear fact — Electrical Americas sales up 20% but operating profit up only 2%, and the 50-basis-point full-year margin guide cut — and you have a fully-priced, consensus-crowded, momentum-favored name whose next forcing event (second-quarter margins, around late July) carries asymmetric downside, not upside. This is a pass for new capital: the narrative is saturated, the multiple has expanded, the catalyst has already run, and the alpha is in the price. Verdict: pass for now, conviction 3 (a 3 rather than 2 reflects franchise quality and a hard backlog floor that makes capital destruction unlikely — this is 'do not chase at the high,' not 'short it'). Value conviction 3: stripping the catalyst and regime overlay and judging purely on valuation against the $410 base-case estimate and forensic quality, Eaton is a wide-moat, high-return compounder trading at to slightly above fair value — fairly valued, no margin of safety, neither cheap nor a trap; a quality hold, not a value buy. The right posture is watchlist: own the quality on a pullback toward the grid and reshoring floor (roughly $340–$360) or after an AI-capex-scare washout, not at 30 times into a fired catalyst.
interrogator_dossier:
# Eaton plc (ETN) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

Prepared for the investment committee, scale-out Basket B (Grid), data as of 2026-06-22. Source: Q1 2026 earnings release and call (reported 2026-05-05); full-year 2025 financials; live quote and valuation.

## Narrative Arc
Eaton's story has compressed from a diversified 'electrification-of-everything' industrial into a sharper 'grid-to-chip' AI-infrastructure play. First-quarter 2026 framing is explicit: data-center orders up roughly 240% year over year, data-center revenue up roughly 50% in Electrical Americas, and positioning as an end-to-end supplier of 'AI factory infrastructure, spanning grid connection, power distribution, and advanced cooling' via the NVIDIA DSX partnership. The March 2026 Boyd Thermal acquisition bolts liquid cooling onto the electrical stack. The megatrend framing widened (reshoring megaprojects up 29% year over year, starts doubled to $54 billion; utility and grid spending) even as the headline story narrowed. The quietly dropped leg is the electric-vehicle business — the Mobility segment fell 6% organically, a deliberate exit from a low-margin line that removes a former growth story.

## Claims vs Financials
Revenue of $7.5 billion, up 17% year over year with 10% organic growth, is corroborated and strong for a company of this scale. Data-center orders up 240%, backlog up 44% and 48%, book-to-bill of 1.2 — the strongest, cleanest facts in the file. On margins: segment margin at 22.7%, down 120 basis points year over year; Electrical Americas operating profit up 2% on 20% higher sales (near-zero incremental margin); full-year segment-margin guide cut by 50 basis points to a range of 24.1–24.5% — this is the key tension. Management's explanation ('temporary price-cost lag fixed by April price increases plus ramp costs') is richer than this quarter's numbers, making it a show-me for the next quarter. Full-year guidance raised by 200 basis points on organic growth to 10%; adjusted earnings per share guided to $13.28 at the midpoint, absorbing Boyd dilution. First-quarter free cash flow of $314 million (up 245% year over year but seasonally light); full-year 2025 free cash flow $3.6 billion. Net assessment: the order and backlog story is fully evidenced; the margin story is where the narrative runs ahead of the reported numbers.

## Tone and Confidence Trajectory
Rising and offensive in tone ('grid-to-chip,' 'twelve-year backlog at current build rates,' '$3.3 trillion megaproject pipeline up 31%'). No defensive macro hedging. The margin guide-down was disclosed plainly with identifiable causes — a level of candor that raises credibility. Trajectory: rising, with a self-aware margin caveat.

## Guidance Credibility
Multi-year record of meeting and raising guidance, with conservative initial guides walked up over the year; the 200-basis-point organic raise after the first quarter fits that pattern. The blemish — a 50-basis-point segment-margin cut — is volume-ramp-driven, making it a higher-quality miss. Reliability: high, with a one-quarter margin asterisk that converts into a second-quarter show-me.

## Analyst Pressure Points
The singular pressure point is incremental margin conversion in Electrical Americas: why did profit not follow orders? Secondary: durability of the 240% data-center order growth (double-ordering and cancellation risk if AI capital-spending guidance is cut). Third: competitive share of the 32 gigawatt pipeline versus Schneider, ABB, Vertiv, and GE Vernova. Management addresses the first question directly, gestures at the second via backlog depth, and sidesteps the third.

## Red Flags and Green Flags
Green: book-to-bill of 1.2 on a 44% higher backlog; honest margin disclosure; differentiated grid-to-chip stack through Boyd and the NVIDIA DSX partnership; free cash flow up 245%, full-year 2025 free cash flow $3.6 billion, $1.9 billion buyback, growing dividend. Red: Electrical Americas operating profit up only 2% on 20% higher sales plus the 50-basis-point margin guide cut; Mobility segment down 6% (electric-vehicle leg retired); valuation at roughly 41 times trailing and roughly 30 times forward earnings on a 10% organic grower; a single shared resolution driver (AI capital-spending durability) that is identical to the rest of the basket.

## Hidden Signals
'Continuous investment rather than large discrete tranches' signals that Eaton is deliberately avoiding the overbuild trap — this protects return on invested capital but caps the speed at which orders convert to profit, and it is itself the source of the ramp costs. The shift to 'grid-to-chip' narrows the story to the highest-multiple, most-crowded end of the market. The Boyd, Fiberband, and Ultra PCS bolt-on acquisitions tilt capital hard toward data-center-adjacent capability — a conviction signal that doubles as a concentration signal. The Mobility exit reframes Eaton as the power-and-thermal supplier for the AI build-out.

## Capital-Allocation Assessment
Trajectory: strengthening. Demand, backlog, book-to-bill, and cash are all inflecting; capital allocation is coherent and concentrating into the highest-return end-market; guidance integrity is high. The single most important consideration: a demonstrably great franchise at a fully-paid price, where the marginal upside is the same bet as the rest of the basket (AI capital-spending durability), at a moment when this quarter's incremental margins disappointed. Moat: scale-cost advantage, switching costs, and intangible value from decades of industrial and utility specifications and brand recognition. Return on invested capital runs roughly 15 percentage points above the cost of capital and is rising. The 120-basis-point margin dip is cyclical and transitional — ramp costs plus price-cost lag with identifiable fixes — not structural. Moat is wide; trend is stable. Secular threat: none — Eaton is on the right side of electrification, AI power demand, grid investment, and reshoring. The only risk is the level and durability of AI capital spending (a demand-cyclical and valuation risk), not terminal impairment. Management credibility 5 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat wide and real; moat trend stable; secular threat none.
skeptic_corrections: Net debt is stale and understated by roughly two times in the original dossier. The valley-of-death section stated 'net debt roughly $9.9 billion' — that is the December 31, 2025 (pre-Boyd) figure. Per the Q1 2026 10-Q balance sheet (filed 2026-05-05, period ending 2026-03-31): total debt is $21.833 billion (long-term $18.535 billion, short-term $2.594 billion, capital leases $0.704 billion), cash and short-term investments $0.751 billion, net debt $21.27 billion. This jump occurred because Eaton funded the $9.5 billion Boyd Thermal cash acquisition with debt (long-term debt rose from $9.40 billion to $18.54 billion; goodwill and intangibles rose from $20.8 billion to $32.7 billion quarter over quarter). This does not break the thesis: $21.3 billion of net debt against roughly $3.6 billion of free cash flow and roughly $11–12 billion of earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization represents roughly 1.7–1.8 times net leverage, which is comfortably investment-grade and well-serviced. The 'no balance-sheet distress' conclusion still holds. However, the 'roughly $9.9 billion, trivially serviced' framing in the file must be updated to roughly $21.3 billion, and the leverage step-up from the debt-funded Boyd acquisition should be flagged explicitly. The bear case is otherwise fully intact and independently verified: the catalyst has already fired (data-center orders up 240%, backlog up 44%, book-to-bill of 1.2 all printed in the first quarter of 2026 and re-rated the stock to roughly 30–32 times forward earnings near its 52-week high of $435.43); the marginal thesis is a single shared factor (AI capital-spending durability) identical to the rest of Basket B; the one hard bear fact — Electrical Americas sales up 20% but operating profit up only 2%, plus the 50-basis-point full-year segment-margin guide cut — is verified; reward-to-risk at $421.77 versus roughly $410 base case is symmetric to unfavorable. The moderator's pass-for-now verdict and watchlist posture are sound.
skeptic_evidence: Eaton Q1-2026 10-Q balance sheet, period 2026-03-31, filed 2026-05-05 (CIK 0001551182): total debt $21.833B (LT $18.535B + ST $2.594B + cap leases $0.704B), cash & ST investments $0.751B => net debt $21.27B; vs 2025-12-31 net debt $10.55B — confirms debt-funded Boyd step-up, NOT the file's ~$9.9B.

Eaton Q1-2026 results (reported 2026-05-05): net sales $7.451B (+17% YoY); data-center orders ~+240% YoY; rolling-12mo book-to-bill 1.2; FY26 organic guide raised +200bps to ~10% (9-11%); adj EPS guide midpoint $13.28 (absorbing Boyd dilution); segment-margin guide CUT ~50bps on Electrical Americas ramp/price-cost lag — all load-bearing bull/bear figures verify.

Eaton press release (eaton.com, 2026-03): completed $9.5B acquisition of Boyd Thermal (liquid cooling, ~22.5x 2026E EBITDA) and unveiled NVIDIA Beam/Vera Rubin DSX 'grid-to-chip' platform — the differentiated power+thermal stack claims confirm against the company IR/primary source.

FMP live quote 2026-06-18 close: ETN $421.77, 52-wk high $435.43, 50d avg $405, mcap ~$163.8B; GuruFocus forward P/E ~31.8x (2026-06-19) — confirms 'near 52-wk high, ~30x fwd, optionality pre-paid / fully priced' (bear's no-mispricing core).

scale.role: Broadest-stack electrical incumbent — power distribution/switchgear into data centers; DC orders +240%, NVDA DSX partner.
scale.rationale: The grid CORE I would seat: most diversified electrical franchise, best balance sheet, broadest stack (now power+thermal via Boyd) — an indispensable toll bordering ~87 on franchise centrality. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS with a material number fix (net debt is ~$21.3B not ~$9.9B after the debt-funded Boyd deal — but at ~1.7-1.8x net leverage it stays comfortably investment-grade, no balance-sheet valley), so a ~12pt haircut. Base ~$410 only ~3% below spot makes it the LEAST-stretched large grid name; the diversification cushions a pure-AI air-pocket.
scale.demand_durability: Very high — electrical distribution is an unavoidable toll; diversified end-markets cushion a pure-AI air-pocket.
